speaker: interviewer
text: Paula Mintz, nee Herling. Paula, when Hitler came to power in 1933, did this have any significance for you and your family in Suchedniow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I recall in '33, no. We were still young, and it wasn't talked about it at home-- I-- which I recall. So I can't tell you much about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what stage did Nazism mean anything to you and you family? And what did it mean to you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the beginning of 1939, they were talking about it. But we didn't know that there will be so fast in-- in the city. There were people who came from other towns, where they-- where they make ghettos, and they send them-- they send them out to different cities. So I remember one family was with us from Plotzk-- was a big Polish city.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jewish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish family. And all-- lots of other Jewish family were also located in Suchedniow with other families. So we were very crowded. And-- and this is before they came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you remember the name of any of these refugees who came to your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they tell you about the anti-Semitism, the anti-Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: They told-- told us very, very awful things. That's why we had such pity on them. No matter how crowded we were, we didn't say anything. And we were with them just like a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these only refugees from Polish cities, or were there refugees from anywhere else?

speaker: interviewee
text: These were from Poland, from Polish. These were from Poland, from different cities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any refugees from Germany who came to your-- your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't know anybody from Germany that came to us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because maybe it was a small-- a small little town, they didn't send them to us. How many could they send to a little town like this for a few families? I don't remember any German people. But I-- when I was in camp, I remember I was with German people in the camp, in Tschenstochau especially. And even so-- it was in such bad conditions-- they still thought that they are the Germans, and they won't do it to them. And they can't do them, such drastic things, what they do to us. They saw it's going-- what's going on, and still they believed, the German citizens, nothing will happen to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before 1939, did you hear of any of the events in Germany, like Kristallnacht? Did you hear about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. And we were very, very upset, I-- I remember. But we could not help it. Time went on, and we had to-- to go on with our lives. But we heard about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you or your family at any time fear that these events would come to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, we did fear. Well, what else is there? We couldn't-- my father was very upset, and he said, we should be in-- on-- on-- in alert for it. But what can we do? We couldn't move out. We didn't have the-- the proper [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] moneys to go. You needed money to-- to travel, and we couldn't. My father always saved the money for dowries for their-- for his daughters. And then the money wasn't so big. We started-- my father bought some leather goods to keep it, because they said the money won't be worth anything. So we had this money put away for that. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you notice any difference towards you on the local Polish population with the increase of Nazism in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. They started to boycott the Jewish stores. And they said only Poles should go to Poles, not to the Jews. And they started-- they started acting up on their side from the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever witness any anti-Jewish violence before the war towards the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't witness specially. In-- during the war, I witnessed that they were very bad to the Jewish people, the Poles, even the ones who knew them. But before it wasn't so bad. So we didn't have any business that they couldn't do anything to us. But the people who had businesses, they felt it. And some people didn't take notice of it. They went to the Jewish stores because they didn't have any other choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the lumber [? mall ?] where you worked, was there any effect?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't see any effect, not at all. Because it was a growing concern. They needed the work. So I didn't see any difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the effect of the outbreak of the war on September the 1st, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, we were devastated. They came in the first day. It was on Friday, September the 1st, 1939. They were already in the-- in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. And we lived, say, it's right from here to-- to across the road, a little further, like the shepherd. There was a railroad, and they bombed the railroad. And we have to move out from our house because all the panes were gone from the windows. And we were, like, naked.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we moved to another family to the woods. They lived in far-- far-- much farther away. And there we stayed for a few days or for a few weeks till it was calm, and they stopped bombing. And we came back. We put in the panes, and we lived there till-- till 1942, when I left home. Was anyone in your family a soldier in the Polish Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the restrictions that came into effect after September 1939 to the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the Jews have to give away the radios first. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A Jew which will have a radio will be shot. So we gave away the radio. And then they told us to give away all the furs and the jewelry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So whatever we had from the furs-- my-- my-- my father had a-- a fur long coat. We gave it away. And we had-- even just collars-- fur collars on our coat, we took off and give them away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't have many-- much jewelry, so we didn't have to give them. But all the Jews, whatever they had. And I remember one incident, my father's brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Vellno. ?] He kept a radio, and they found it in his place.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans found it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans found it. And they took him out to shoot, and they shot him. That was a terrible tragedy to our family. When we heard it, it was devastating. And from then on, we-- we saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness that, Paula?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not witness it. I saw it before I went down to see him. It was terrible, a terrible shock. And I don't know what happens further after. And this-- this incident I remember vividly, that they found that radio in the-- in his place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the effect on the family when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Oh, ?] terrible, terrible. So they-- they went-- he had six sons. So the son went-- the sons went on hiding. Three sons went on hiding. So they-- they found the hiding place later, and they took them out and they shot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not three. They shot two, and one was alive. They didn't see the-- the third one. And these people-- and one-- one is survived. He was hiding in a big city as a barber. And he looked like a gentile, so he survived the war. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names? Itzhak Mayer, Moshe [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: All Herling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cousins, Herling. So one survive, and one was-- was hidden as a barber in a big city survived, too. But they were-- till this day they never re-married. They were depressed and couldn't get on with life-- what they saw, what they witnessed. They still live in New York State, and I write to them sometimes. They-- they write to me. But they never married because of what they saw. They were in a terrible, terrible state.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the other restrictions, anti-Jewish restrictions, that the Germans brought into effect in Suchedniow?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your life change?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Later, ?] took everybody to work for them. And they cut the beards from all the men, the religious men, everyone, and their beard. So they cut it. And they didn't cut it straight. They cut it with a piece of flesh, too. That's why I didn't, and our sisters, didn't let our fathers out from the house. Wherever-- if he needed to go, we went with them. I went with my other sister. And if somebody came up, we pleaded with the Germans to let them go. We cried for them. And some of-- they had a little pity. That was the Wehrmacht, not the SS. That was only the soldiers. So they let us go. But alone, my father never left this house, and he was very depressed. And he couldn't make a living because everything was confiscated. And so I did, and with my sister, work different jobs of work. We were peddling different merchandise. Getting from somebody, going around and peddling it. And that's how we made it. We made a living till I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your non-Jewish friends-- what was their reaction to what was taking place in Suchedniow?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were afraid too. They were afraid too. Their daughters came to us sometimes to overnight sleeping. I-- I know my girlfriends, they came to our-- because they said that they're going to-- to make a-- an [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know what this is in-- in English. But they search for-- for girls. So-- and they were nice girls, good looking. So they-- they were afraid. Jewish girls, they couldn't have because this wasn't-- they weren't Aryans. So they didn't do anything to Jewish girls, except very seldom they did something. So the-- they were very-- they were also shot, if one did have any business with a Jewish girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would be shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. But they had business with the Polish girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was this looked upon by the Polish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish population, they were also very rotten, because the Germans didn't-- didn't recognize the Jewish people when they came in. They didn't recognize a Jew or the Pole. But the Poles came with the Germans, and they pointed out who was a Jew. And they told the Poles if they will do this telling who the Jews are, they will get a kilo of sugar or something for that. So they did that. And the Jews were very afraid of them, of the Poles, never mind of the Germans. It was a awful-- awful situation. And I-- I-- I know a fact where my neighbor-- they had two daughters, and they were rich. And they always have business with gentiles. So they paid them money. They should hide them. And they went to hiding. They took away the whole-- the money, and they shot them, and nobody survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Farman. ?] That-- them-- them I know, I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were shot, the whole family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole family, nobody survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the-- the Polish people weren't nice to us. Very terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the effect on the Jewish life, the Jewish, the shuls, the newspapers? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They didn't do anything to them. Nothing. Nobody was-- was punished for that. I don't think anybody was punished for that. They were allowed to do whatever they want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear any identification as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. We all had to to wear yellow stars on the arms, on the right arm. And I did have. I even have a picture with a star we took. So if one didn't have, also was shot. So that we were recognized after-- after a while. They didn't-- they didn't have to point out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any of these shootings--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and violence towards the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in-- not here, not in the [? Suchedniow. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: You had younger brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they remain at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My-- my brothers were older. My sisters were younger. And they were still in-- but all finished seven classes. My youngest in Montreal, Bella, she had just finished the-- the same year, what the war broke out, she finished seven class. So she was in her 13 and 1/2 year when she went to camp, or 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: And other Jewish children who were at school, did they continue to go to school under German occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They weren't going to school. I don't remember one went to school. Schools were shot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't remember if the-- if the Polish people went to school. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the Jewish communal leaders in Suchedniow?

speaker: interviewee
text: There weren't any leaders during the war. No. Nobody had the special-- specific job to be a leader, like in the big ghettos. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it come about that the ghetto was established in Suchedniow?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just said on loudspeakers, and there's some-- some notices on the walls that all the Jews have to remain where they are. They can't move around, turn about, any other-- other street out of this in this district. So the Jews knew. And there was a curfew also, not to go after a certain hour. So that's what we knew from-- from the notices and the loudspeakers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family have to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't. We didn't move. We remained in the same house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would have happened to anybody had they been out during curfew or gone out of the ghetto in the non-Jew--

speaker: interviewee
text: They would be punished severely, [INAUDIBLE] shot, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't witness anything, but we heard of incidents. That's why people were very afraid to go out. We were very-- we were very obedient. We didn't go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a fence of any nature around this area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I live?

speaker: interviewer
text: The [? so-called ?] ghetto-- was there a fence or a-- a wall around it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there guards-- German guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But if they saw somebody not in their facility, in their district, they did-- they knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did this take place that you were instructed to remain within an area?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it started around '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what time of year this took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember. It's going to be soon 50 years. I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember many names, just the most immediate family and-- and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear of what was going on in the camps in Poland--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --while you were in Suchedniow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I heard because at first they went to camp. And the-- and trucks brought them home over-- overnight. During the day, they worked there, and they came home. But all of a sudden, they stopped that. They didn't bring them home. They held them overnight and for-- forever. So then I decided I will go to-- to my sisters, to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to spend a little bit more time talking about living in the ghetto, as such. How did this affect your family life?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family life-- it affect so far that we couldn't go out during in the-- the curfew and to the other streets. But we lived what on our-- what we had, very poorly, very poorly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear anything about the extermination camps in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because when I was home, a guy escaped from Treblinka, and I remember like now. And he came over to us, so that a neighbor of mine, that farmer, he knew him. And he said, people, don't believe that they take us-- take you to the-- to work. They take you to Treblinka, and they finished you off in the ovens. But we didn't believe it. We did not believe it that one can-- can come to that-- do it. And he left. But that was the true story. He said the truth. Later on, we learned that this was the truth. And we didn't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your family during this period? Did you separate? Were there any transports from Suchedniow?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family? No. Till I left home, we were all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did this happen? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened is they came from the same camp where my sisters were.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your sisters get to that camp, and what camp was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was ammunition company-- factory in Skarzysko-Kamienna near Kielce. And there-- there was a Polish ammunition factory before the war. And they established theirs over there. And they kept people there, first voluntarily. Come to work. So my two sisters went as volunteers, and then they didn't let them go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sisters? What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine-- my-- youngest sisters. One was Sally. One was Bella. They were there. So next time the volunteer trucks came and they said they need more workers. Who wants to volunteer? If not, you'll go to-- to-- to die. You'll go away from here. So I wanted to go to help them. First, I didn't want to leave my mother and father and the rest of the family. So my father was very panicky. He started begging me and pleaded with me. We are older people. Whatever will be with us will be, but you are young people. Go and save yourself. Maybe you will live through it, that you will-- [SIGHS] you'll be-- live over the war, and you'll be alive. Go. And he banged his head in the wall and cried terribly like a child. I couldn't see it. I didn't even say good-bye. And I left my house and went on the track with my sister Chajka. She was the oldest, and she lived somewhere else. But she heard that too, and she came on the track. And we came to the-- to the Skarzysko-Kamienna [INAUDIBLE]. I-- and so I said I will be with my sisters. Maybe I'll help them survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your elder sister Chajka, was she married?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was married, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she left her husband and children? When was this that you left Suchedniow to go to Skarzysko?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was about the middle of August 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you transported to Skarzysko-Kamienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the big trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many trucks were there in the transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few, a few trucks came, and they all were full. People were volunteering.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately how many people were there per truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a hundred for sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did the journey take?

speaker: interviewee
text: It doesn't take long, an hour perhaps, or less. Because it was nine miles away from our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the guards on the trucks? Were there guards on the trucks?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there guards on the trucks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Guards, yes, soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What nationality were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans. German in uniforms. And when I came there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you left, do you remember what your parting words with your father and your mother were?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left Suchedniow. Do you remember the last words that you said to your parents, that they said to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what they said. Go, save your lives. We are old. Maybe you are young. You'll survive. And they send us all. But my sister didn't-- she said she's not going to work for the Germans. She's not going-- not going to be with the German. She's going to the woods and be a partisan. She will do something to kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The older one from me, two years older, Nacha. And I don't know, she probably left. But I didn't hear from her, and I didn't know what the-- the end was. And my eldest sister, Miriam [? Esther, ?] she was married with one little child, a year old. So she went to the Treblinka with my parents. She couldn't go away.
